title: TOP SECRET: The Moscow File
subtitle: Do not trust the official version.
description: A declassified historical investigation through central Moscow.
cover_caption:
TOP SECRET: THE MOSCOW FILE

Do not trust the official version.

Case No. 1147-M
Declassified Field Investigation
route_summary: Peter the Great Monument → Patriarchal Bridge → Red October → House on the Embankment → Udarnik → Bersenevka → Bolotnaya Square → Vodootvodny Canal → Tretyakov Gallery
final_phrase: MOSCOW DOES NOT HIDE ITS SECRETS UNDERGROUND. IT LEAVES THEM IN PUBLIC.
start_message:
Do not trust the official version.

Former classification: TOP SECRET
Case No.: 1147-M
Status: declassified
Access: temporary
Assignment: field investigation
Mission: restore the missing report

You are not tourists.

You are a field group assigned to review a declassified Moscow file that was closed without a final conclusion.

The file was discovered in restricted archive storage.
Its cover was damaged.
Several pages were missing.
Names were blacked out.
Dates were corrected by hand.
The final protocol was never signed.

Only one warning remained clearly visible on the first page:

DO NOT TRUST THE OFFICIAL VERSION.

According to the file, nine coordinates in central Moscow form a route connected with repeated historical distortions: monuments, erased buildings, renamed factories, controlled spaces, public rituals, water engineering, and works of art that refused to stay silent.

The original investigator described Moscow as:

“a city that does not destroy evidence — it builds over it.”

Your task is to complete the investigation.

Type START to open the field protocol.
rules_message:
Field protocol:

Reach the coordinate.
Press ARRIVED.
Read the classified fragment.
Submit one short answer.

Use hints carefully.
Every hint and every wrong answer leaves a trace in the file.
Your final investigator profile will depend on how clean your protocol is.

Safety rules:
Stay together.
Use only public spaces.
Do not enter restricted areas or private courtyards.
Cross roads only at safe crossings.
Do not run near water.
Respect other visitors, workers, guards and local residents.

Type I AGREE to proceed.
safety_message:
Field protocol:

Reach the coordinate.
Press ARRIVED.
Read the classified fragment.
Submit one short answer.

Use hints carefully.
Every hint and every wrong answer leaves a trace in the file.
Your final investigator profile will depend on how clean your protocol is.

Safety rules:
Stay together.
Use only public spaces.
Do not enter restricted areas or private courtyards.
Cross roads only at safe crossings.
Do not run near water.
Respect other visitors, workers, guards and local residents.

Type I AGREE to proceed.
organizer_note: Students restore redacted report lines. Answers are short English words. The quest is historical and cultural, not vocabulary-based. Launch note: treat the Repin entry-ban story as a famous gallery anecdote unless separately verified.
finish_message:
FINAL REPORT RESTORED

The official version was incomplete.

Moscow was not erased once.
It was rewritten again and again.

A ruler imported more than ships.
A sacred site changed its body.
A factory carried imperial symbolism before revolution renamed it.
A red fortress kept a white memory.
A cinema learned to speak.
An old city was disciplined through appearance.
A square turned rebellion into imperial warning.
A canal changed the shape of the city.
A painter refused to let memory stay finished.

Conclusion:

Moscow does not hide its secrets underground.
It leaves them in public.

On walls.
In names.
In water.
In buildings.
In paintings.
In places people pass without looking.

The file is restored.

Now the protocol will judge how clean your investigation was.

auto_profiles.ROOKIE_OBSERVER:
STATUS: FILE REVIEWED, EVIDENCE INCOMPLETE

You completed the route, but your protocol contains many traces: hints, wrong entries, and uncertain reconstructions.

You saw the surface of the case, but some hidden links remained in the archive shadow.

Still, the investigation has begun.

You now know that Moscow’s landmarks are never just landmarks.
auto_profiles.URBAN_DECODER:
STATUS: PATTERN DETECTED

You restored enough fragments to see the main pattern:

Moscow repeatedly rewrites itself, but the evidence survives.

You noticed that buildings, monuments, canals, squares, and galleries can all work as historical documents.

Your protocol is not perfectly clean, but the central logic of the file is clear.
auto_profiles.MOSCOW_INVESTIGATOR:
STATUS: CASE RECONSTRUCTED

You connected the route into a coherent investigation.

You understood the hidden structure: power, reform, religion, fear, infrastructure, cinema, punishment, and art all leave traces in the city.

Your protocol shows strong field judgment.

You did not just visit Moscow.

You read it.
auto_profiles.ARCHIVE_MASTER:
STATUS: CLASSIFIED-LEVEL INTERPRETATION

Your protocol is almost clean.

You restored the file with minimal uncertainty and detected the deeper mechanism of the case:

Moscow does not erase the past completely.

It builds over it, renames it, restores it, and accidentally preserves it.

For you, the city is no longer a map of attractions.

It is an archive of contradictions.

locations.title: COORDINATE 01 — The Giant by the River
locations.address: Krymskaya Embankment / western confluence of the Moskva River and Vodootvodny Canal
locations.message:
COORDINATE 01 CONFIRMED

The file opens with a water stain across the first page.

The giant above you does not look like he belongs to the river. He looks like he is forcing the river to obey.

Case note:
The official version points to Peter as a ruler of ships, reforms, and imperial ambition.

But the file does not stop at the ship.

What the official version misses:
Peter’s reforms did not only change armies, ports, and state institutions. They entered everyday life: clothes, habits, food, manners, and even what people were expected to accept as “modern.”

Evidence on site:
Look at the monument. It is oversized, theatrical, almost foreign to the city around it.

The clue is not only about the sea.
It is about how Peter tried to import a new world into Russia.
locations.question:
Redacted line:
“Peter’s reforms are usually remembered through ships, uniforms, and state power. But the file records a quieter invasion: a foreign crop that later became ordinary Russian food — the ______.”

Restore the missing word.
hints: It later became one of the most common foods in Russia.
It grows underground and is often boiled, fried, or mashed.
locations.wrong_reply:
ENTRY NOT CONFIRMED.

The file does not point to a ship this time.
Think about Peter’s reforms entering everyday life, not only state power.
locations.success_reply:
PAGE 01 RESTORED.

The official version was too narrow.

Modernization was not only about ships, armies, and borders.
It could enter the table, the wardrobe, the face, the street, and the habits of everyday life.
locations.speaking_task:
🎙️ Speaking Task:
Why can changes in food, clothing, and daily habits be more difficult for society than changes in laws or institutions?
locations.next_directions: Use the map link to reach the first coordinate: Peter the Great Monument by the river. When your team is at the location, press ARRIVED.
locations.destination_message:
NEXT DESTINATION

locations.title: COORDINATE 02 — The Site with Three Lives
locations.location_name: Patriarchal Bridge viewpoint / Cathedral of Christ the Saviour
locations.address: Patriarshy Bridge, view toward Cathedral of Christ the Saviour
locations.message:
COORDINATE 02 CONFIRMED

The second page is damaged by water.

At first glance, the official version looks complete: a cathedral, a landmark, a restored symbol.

But the file disagrees.

Case note:
This place changed identities more than once.

Sacred ground became a demolition site.
Then it became the planned location of a Soviet mega-project.
Then something even stranger appeared here.

What the official version misses:
The site was not simply destroyed and later restored.

It was overwritten.

Evidence on site:
Look at the cathedral.

Now imagine the least cathedral-like public facility possible occupying this same ground.
locations.question:
Redacted line:
“The official version shows a cathedral. The file records a place that was destroyed, planned as a Soviet palace, and then filled not with prayers, but with swimmers: a ______.”

Restore the missing word.
hints: It was a public facility connected with water.
locations.wrong_reply:
ENTRY NOT CONFIRMED.

This place had an unexpectedly ordinary life before the cathedral returned.
Check the fragment again.
locations.success_reply:
PAGE 02 RESTORED.

The official version was incomplete.

This place was sacred, Soviet, recreational, and sacred again.
Moscow did not erase the past cleanly — it stacked one version over another.
locations.speaking_task:
🎙️ Speaking Task:
What does the transformation from cathedral to Soviet project to swimming pool and back to cathedral show about religious memory and Soviet ideology?
locations.next_directions: Proceed to the viewpoint near Patriarchal Bridge and face the Cathedral of Christ the Saviour.
locations.destination_message:
NEXT DESTINATION

COORDINATE 02 — The Site with Three Lives

📍 Location: Patriarchal Bridge viewpoint / Cathedral of Christ the Saviour

📌 Address / point: Patriarshy Bridge, view toward Cathedral of Christ the Saviour

🗺️ Yandex Maps:

https://yandex.ru/maps/?text=%D0%9F%D0%B0%D1%82%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B0%D1%80%D1%88%D0%B8%D0%B9%20%D0%BC%D0%BE%D1%81%D1%82%20%D0%A5%D1%80%D0%B0%D0%BC%20%D0%A5%D1%80%D0%B8%D1%81%D1%82%D0%B0%20%D0%A1%D0%BF%D0%B0%D1%81%D0%B8%D1%82%D0%B5%D0%BB%D1%8F%20%D0%9C%D0%BE%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B2%D0%B0

Proceed toward the former Red October Factory on Bersenevskaya Embankment.

locations.title: COORDINATE 03 — The Red-Brick Disguise
locations.message:
COORDINATE 03 CONFIRMED

The red-brick walls match the archive photograph.

The file does not smell of smoke or metal here.
It smells faintly of sugar, paper wrappers, and a name changed after the revolution.

Case note:
Today this area may look like post-industrial Moscow: creative spaces, cafés, offices, galleries.

But the file records another layer:
factory Moscow, commercial Moscow, branded Moscow.

What the official version misses:
Before the area became fashionable, it produced something that entered ordinary life through shops, gifts, wrappers, childhood memory, and Soviet shelves.

Evidence on site:
Look at the factory architecture.

This was not a palace of power.
It was a machine for producing desire.
locations.question:
Redacted line:
“When the Einem factory received the status of supplier to the Imperial Court, its packaging could carry the empire’s most recognizable symbol: the double-headed ______.”

Restore the missing word.
hints: The missing word is an animal used in imperial symbolism.
It is a bird with two heads.
locations.wrong_reply:
ENTRY NOT CONFIRMED.

The file does not ask what the factory produced.
It asks what imperial symbol could appear on products connected with court supplier status.
locations.success_reply:
PAGE 03 RESTORED.

The file confirms it:

This was not only factory Moscow.
It was status, taste, branding, and imperial symbolism — later renamed by revolution.
locations.speaking_task:
🎙️ Speaking Task:
Why would a business want to place a state symbol on its products, and how does this connect commerce with political power?
locations.next_directions: Proceed toward the former Red October Factory on Bersenevskaya Embankment.
locations.destination_message:
NEXT DESTINATION

locations.title: COORDINATE 04 — The Fortress That Changed Color
locations.location_name: House on the Embankment / Kremlin viewpoint
locations.address: Serafimovicha Street, 2 / view toward the Kremlin
locations.message:
COORDINATE 04 CONFIRMED

The page opens with a color correction.

You see one of the most recognizable images of Moscow: red walls, towers, state power, official ceremonies.

The archive asks a different question.

Case note:
The official version shows the Kremlin as red — almost as if it has always looked this way.

But the file marks this color as unstable.

What the official version misses:
For a long period, the Kremlin walls were covered with limewash.

The red fortress had another public face.

Evidence on site:
Look toward the Kremlin.

The red color feels permanent.
The archive suggests it is not.
locations.question:
Redacted line:
“The symbol looks permanent because power wants symbols to look permanent. But the archive records another face of the Kremlin: for generations, its walls appeared ______.”

Restore the missing word.
hints: The clue is not in the brick itself, but in what covered the brick for a long time.
The walls were covered with limewash.
locations.wrong_reply:
ENTRY NOT CONFIRMED.

The file does not ask what color the Kremlin is today.
It asks what color it appeared for many generations.
locations.success_reply:
PAGE 04 RESTORED.

The official version has been corrected.

Even Moscow’s most familiar symbols were not always seen the way we see them now.

The red fortress had a white memory.
locations.speaking_task:
🎙️ Speaking Task:
Why do later visual images often replace older historical memory in people’s minds?
locations.next_directions: Proceed toward the House on the Embankment.
locations.destination_message:
NEXT DESTINATION

Route note:

Proceed to Udarnik Cinema.

When your team arrives at the point, press ARRIVED.

locations.title: COORDINATE 05 — The Hall of the New Signal
locations.message:
COORDINATE 05 CONFIRMED

The page opens like a dark hall before the projector starts.

A public hall.
A screen.
A crowd looking in the same direction.

Case note:
The official version says: cinema.

The file says: signal.

What the official version misses:
In the 1930s, cinema was not only entertainment.

It could organize attention, emotion, ideology, and collective memory.

Evidence on site:
Imagine the hall full.

One screen.
Hundreds of people.
The same image.
The same rhythm.
The same command to listen.
locations.question:
Redacted line:
“The cinema did not only add entertainment. In a state that wanted to speak to millions at once, the screen became more powerful when it acquired ______.”

Restore the missing word.
hints: Earlier films could move, but they could not do this.
It changed cinema from something only seen into something also heard.
locations.wrong_reply:
ENTRY NOT CONFIRMED.

Think about the difference between silent cinema and the next technological stage.
locations.success_reply:
PAGE 05 RESTORED.

The file confirms the shift:

Moscow’s modernity was not only built in stone.
It was projected, amplified, and heard by crowds.
locations.speaking_task:
🎙️ Speaking Task:
Why was sound especially important for Soviet mass culture and propaganda?
locations.destination_message:
NEXT DESTINATION

COORDINATE 05 — The Hall of the New Signal

📍 Location: Udarnik Cinema

📌 Address / point: Serafimovicha Street, 2

🗺️ Yandex Maps:

https://yandex.ru/maps/?text=%D0%9A%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%BE%D1%82%D0%B5%D0%B0%D1%82%D1%80%20%D0%A3%D0%B4%D0%B0%D1%80%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%BA%20%D0%9C%D0%BE%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B2%D0%B0%20%D0%A1%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%84%D0%B8%D0%BC%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B8%D1%87%D0%B0%202

Route note:

Proceed toward the old Moscow layer near St. Nicholas on Bersenevka.

When your team arrives at the point, press ARRIVED.

locations.title: COORDINATE 06 — The Old Layer Under the Route
locations.location_name: Church of St. Nicholas on Bersenevka / old estate area
locations.message:
COORDINATE 06 CONFIRMED

The route turns older here.

The page becomes quieter:
stone, church walls, private courtyards, old habits.

This is the Moscow that reform did not simply replace.
It inspected it, measured it, and disciplined it.

Case note:
The official version sees an old church and historic architecture.

The file sees a surviving fragment of pre-reform Moscow.

What the official version misses:
This corner belongs to a world of private estates, religious habits, traditional appearance, and old social codes.

It is the kind of world Peter wanted to change.

Evidence on site:
Look for the older layer.

This stop is not only about buildings.
It is about the Moscow that reform tried to edit.
locations.question:
Redacted line:
“Peter’s reforms reached the army, the calendar, clothing, and manners. But one tax made modernization visible on the human face itself: the tax on the ______.”

Restore the missing word.
hints: The reform was about appearance, not architecture.
It was something many traditional Russian men wore on their faces.
locations.wrong_reply:
ENTRY NOT CONFIRMED.

The clue is not the church itself, but the old Moscow lifestyle that Peter tried to reform.
locations.success_reply:
PAGE 06 RESTORED.

The file confirms:

Reform was not only political.
It was visual, social, and personal.

Moscow’s old identity was edited even on people’s faces.
locations.speaking_task:
🎙️ Speaking Task:
Why did Peter the Great care so much about external appearance?
locations.next_directions: Proceed toward the old Moscow layer near St. Nicholas on Bersenevka.
locations.destination_message:
NEXT DESTINATION

Route note:

Proceed to Bolotnaya Square.

When your team arrives at the point, press ARRIVED.

locations.title: COORDINATE 07 - The Rebel’s Last Stage
locations.message:
COORDINATE 07 CONFIRMED

Open space.
Quiet surface.
Unquiet record.

The file marks this square not as empty land, but as a place where power once needed witnesses.

Case note:
The official version sees a public square.

The file sees a stage.

What the official version misses:
In earlier Moscow, punishment was not always hidden behind walls.

It could be shown to a crowd as a warning.

Evidence on site:
Look at the openness of the square.

Imagine people gathered here not for a walk, not for a market, not for a celebration.

Imagine them gathered to watch power turn law into spectacle.
locations.question:
Redacted line:
“In 1775, one rebel’s death turned this square into a warning from the empire. His name was ______.”

Restore the missing name.
hints: His uprising took place during the reign of Catherine II.
locations.wrong_reply:
ENTRY NOT CONFIRMED.

Think of the rebel leader whose uprising challenged Catherine II’s empire.
locations.success_reply:
PAGE 07 RESTORED.

The file records another hidden layer:

Bolotnaya Square was not only an open urban space.
It could become a stage where the empire turned rebellion, punishment, and public fear into one political message.
locations.speaking_task:
🎙️ Speaking Task:
Why did empires use the public punishment of rebels as a form of political communication?
locations.destination_message:
NEXT DESTINATION

COORDINATE 07 — The Square That Remembered Crowds

📍 Location: Bolotnaya Square

📌 Address / point: Bolotnaya Square, Moscow

Route note:

locations.title: COORDINATE 08 — The Edited River
locations.location_name: Vodootvodny Canal / crossing toward Lavrushinsky Lane
locations.address: Vodootvodny Canal area, near Luzhkov Bridge
locations.message:
COORDINATE 08 CONFIRMED

The file switches from stone to water.

Here the city stops pretending history is only made by rulers.

Sometimes history is made by mud, spring water, and the fear that the river will not stay where it belongs.

Case note:
The official version calls this a canal.

The archive calls it an intervention.

What the official version misses:
Moscow was not only built upward in stone.

It was also corrected, drained, redirected, and protected from recurring natural threats.

Evidence on site:
Look at the water.

Then look at the map.

The canal is not decoration.
It is an engineered answer to a problem.
locations.question:
Redacted line:
“The canal did not simply move water. Together with the Moskva River, it changed the map itself and turned this part of the city into an ______.”

Restore the missing word.
hints: It was especially dangerous for low areas near the river.
locations.wrong_reply:
ENTRY NOT CONFIRMED.

The canal was a practical response to a recurring water-related danger.
locations.success_reply:
PAGE 08 RESTORED.

The file confirms:

Moscow’s memory is not only in monuments.
It is also in infrastructure — in the ways the city survived water, mud, and danger.
locations.speaking_task:
🎙️ Speaking Task:
Why should infrastructure be studied as part of political and urban history?
locations.destination_message:
NEXT DESTINATION

COORDINATE 08 — The Edited River

📍 Location: Vodootvodny Canal / crossing toward Lavrushinsky Lane

📌 Address / point: Vodootvodny Canal area, near Luzhkov Bridge

Route note:

Proceed to the State Tretyakov Gallery.

When your team arrives at the point, press ARRIVED.

locations.title: COORDINATE 09 — The Restless Painter
locations.message:
COORDINATE 09 CONFIRMED

Final page detected.

At first, this looks like the safest place in the file:
a gallery, a collection, a museum of national art.

But the last note is written almost like a warning:

even finished memory can be edited.

Case note:
The official version says paintings preserve memory.

The file asks:
what if memory refuses to stay finished?

What the official version misses:
Even after a work enters a collection, it can remain unstable — especially if its creator cannot stop returning to it.

Evidence on site:
Look at the gallery as an archive of images.

Then read the warning:
even a finished painting may not be completely safe.
locations.question:
Redacted line:
“The final page contains the strangest warning in the file: even after a painting entered the gallery, it was not always safe from its creator. The artist who kept returning to correct his own works was ______.”

Restore the missing name.
hints: He is one of the most famous Russian realist painters of the 19th century.
He painted Ivan the Terrible and His Son Ivan and They Did Not Expect Him.
locations.wrong_reply:
ENTRY NOT CONFIRMED.

Think of a major Russian realist painter famous for constantly revising his own works.
locations.success_reply:
FINAL PAGE RESTORED.

Even a museum can become a crime scene when an artist refuses to stop editing his own masterpieces.

The file is complete.
locations.speaking_task:
🎙️ Speaking Task:
What does Repin’s habit of correcting finished works reveal about the relationship between artist, artwork, collector, and public memory?
locations.next_directions: Proceed to the State Tretyakov Gallery.
locations.destination_message:
NEXT DESTINATION

COORDINATE 09 — The Restless Painter

📍 Location: State Tretyakov Gallery
